publishers from abruptly disabling access to games that individuals have paid for, particularly when those games rely on online authentication or centralized servers.

paragraphs.text: As digital media and entertainment become increasingly central to everyday life, it is vital that legislation like the Digital Fairness Act addresses the growing concerns around digital ownership, longevity of access, and consumer rightsparticularly in the realm of video games. One critical issue that merits more robust protection is the delisting and shutdown of video games, especially those that consumers have legally purchased or invested significant time and money into. The recent public response to the Stop Destroying Videogames initiative reflects a growing frustration among players regarding the removal of access to games without adequate notice, refunds, or preservation options. We respectfully urge lawmakers to ensure that the Digital Fairness Act includes provisions that prevent publishers from abruptly disabling access to games that individuals have paid for, particularly when those games rely on online authentication or centralized servers. Consumers should have the right to continue accessing and playing the products they legally acquired, even after a game's official support ends. There is currently a troubling trend in which major publishers shut down online-only games without offering alternatives, such as an offline mode or community-hosted server options. This practice often leads to complete loss of access, even for single-player content, effectively erasing the investment and enjoyment of countless players. Notable examples include: Ubisofts The Crew: Despite being a paid title, it was rendered entirely unplayable in 2024 due to server shutdowns. EAs Mirrors Edge Catalyst: Although primarily a single-player game, it has faced functionality issues after backend features were disabled. Various mobile games with substantial microtransaction investment have also been taken offline with little recourse for players. We believe the Digital Fairness Act should: Require publishers to provide reasonable advance notice before deactivating access to a game. Mandate that if a game is to be discontinued, efforts must be made to enable offline functionality where possible. Support preservation efforts, including exemptions for archival and community-run servers, especially for games no longer commercially exploited. Include clear refund policies or credit options for recent purchases of games scheduled for imminent shutdown. Such measures would not only protect consumers but also promote accountability and long-term trust in the digital marketplace. Digital products should not be disposable, and players should not lose access to legally obtained content due to unilateral decisions by publishers. In conclusion, we urge the authors of the Digital Fairness Act to consider the perspective of everyday players who have experienced the loss of cherished games without meaningful alternatives or protections. A fair digital future should honor the value of digital ownership and safeguard it against unnecessary erasure.
